page: The Vultr vx1-m-96c-768gb is a virtual machine instance within the VX1 Cloud Compute family. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an AMD processor, delivering 96 virtual CPUs and 96 physical cores with a single thread per core configuration. The system is equipped with 768.0 GB of system memory, providing a ratio of 8.0 GB of RAM per core. Storage is provisioned via network-attached storage with a capacity of 1 GB, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No dedicated GPU hardware is present on this server type.

A key architectural characteristic of this instance is its shared CPU allocation. While it offers a massive allocation of 96 cores and 768.0 GB of RAM, the shared CPU model introduces potential resource contention, making it less suitable for workloads requiring sustained, predictable maximum CPU performance. From a cost-efficiency perspective, this shared resource model allows Vultr to offer high memory and core density at a lower qualitative price point compared to dedicated-core instances of similar scale. It represents a tradeoff between raw resource capacity and guaranteed compute performance.

Given the high memory capacity and shared CPU architecture, this instance is best suited for memory-intensive workloads that experience bursty or non-continuous CPU utilization. Appropriate use cases include large-scale caching layers, in-memory databases with variable query loads, staging environments for big data analytics, and general-purpose applications requiring substantial memory footprints without continuous peak CPU demands.
description: The Vultr vx1-m-96c-768gb is an x86_64 virtual server from the VX1 Cloud Compute family, configured with 96 AMD vCPUs and 96 physical cores operating at one thread per core. It features a substantial memory capacity of 768.0 GB, yielding 8.0 GB of RAM per core, alongside 1 GB of network-attached storage and a single public IPv4 address. The instance utilizes a shared CPU allocation model, which offers high resource density and qualitative cost efficiency for memory-heavy workloads, though it introduces potential CPU resource contention. Lacking dedicated GPU hardware, this server is designed for memory-intensive applications with variable CPU demands, such as large in-memory databases, caching systems, and big data staging environments.
